args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get the business data from the previous query
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:6']

# Initialize state-level aggregators
state_review_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Total reviews per state
state_rating_sums = defaultdict(float)  # Sum of ratings per state
state_business_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Count of businesses per state
business_to_state = {}  # Map business_id to state

# First pass: extract states from description and aggregate review counts
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    review_count = int(business['review_count'])
    
    # Extract state from description
    description = business.get('description', '')
    state_match = re.search(r'\b([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    
    if state_match:
        state = state_match.group(1)
        business_to_state[business_id] = state
        state_review_counts[state] += review_count
        state_business_counts[state] += 1

# Now get review data from user_database
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'state_review_counts': dict(state_review_counts),
    'state_business_counts': dict(state_business_counts)
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get the business data - using the most recent query result
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:13']

# Debug: print how many businesses we have
print(f"DEBUG: Number of businesses: {len(business_data)}")
print(f"DEBUG: First business: {business_data[0] if business_data else 'No data'}")

# Initialize state-level aggregators
state_review_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Total reviews per state
state_business_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Count of businesses per state
business_to_state = {}  # Map business_id to state

# Extract states and aggregate review counts
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    review_count = int(business['review_count'])
    
    # Extract state from description (e.g., "CA" from "Located at ... in Goleta, CA")
    description = business.get('description', '')
    state_match = re.search(r'\b([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    
    if state_match:
        state = state_match.group(1)
        business_to_state[business_id] = state
        state_review_counts[state] += review_count
        state_business_counts[state] += 1

# Debug: show what we found
print(f"DEBUG: States found: {dict(state_review_counts)}")

# Return as JSON
result = {
    'state_review_counts': dict(state_review_counts),
    'state_business_counts': dict(state_business_counts),
    'business_to_state': dict(business_to_state)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Read the business data file
business_file = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    business_data = json.load(f)

# Initialize state-level aggregators
state_review_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Total reviews per state
state_business_ids = defaultdict(list)  # Business IDs per state
business_to_state = {}  # Map business_id to state

# Extract states and aggregate review counts
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    review_count = int(business['review_count'])
    
    # Extract state from description (e.g., "CA" from "Located at ... in Goleta, CA")
    description = business.get('description', '')
    state_match = re.search(r'\b([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    
    if state_match:
        state = state_match.group(1)
        business_to_state[business_id] = state
        state_review_counts[state] += review_count
        state_business_ids[state].append(business_id)

# Find state with highest number of reviews
if state_review_counts:
    top_state = max(state_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
else:
    top_state = (None, 0)

result = {
    'state_review_counts': dict(state_review_counts),
    'top_state': top_state[0],
    'top_state_review_count': top_state[1],
    'businesses_in_top_state': state_business_ids.get(top_state[0], []),
    'total_businesses': len(business_data)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get business data directly from storage
business_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Initialize state-level aggregators
state_review_counts = defaultdict(int)  # Total reviews per state
state_business_ids = defaultdict(list)  # Business IDs per state
business_to_state = {}  # Map business_id to state

# Extract states and aggregate review counts
for business in business_data:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    review_count = int(business['review_count'])
    
    # Extract state from description (e.g., "CA" from "Located at ... in Goleta, CA")
    description = business.get('description', '')
    state_match = re.search(r'\b([A-Z]{2})\b', description)
    
    if state_match:
        state = state_match.group(1)
        business_to_state[business_id] = state
        state_review_counts[state] += review_count
        state_business_ids[state].append(business_id)

# Find state with highest number of reviews
top_state = None
top_state_review_count = 0
if state_review_counts:
    top_state, top_state_review_count = max(state_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])

result = {
    'state_review_counts': dict(state_review_counts),
    'top_state': top_state,
    'top_state_review_count': top_state_review_count,
    'businesses_in_top_state': state_business_ids.get(top_state, []),
    'total_businesses': len(business_data)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:5.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
